food_name: Laksa Johor (Johorean fish-coconut laksa with thick noodles)
food_subgroup: Rice noodles or spaghetti-style noodles in fish-coconut curry gravy

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Laksa Johor. Closest parent is fish-based coconut curry noodle dish. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid code fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice or wheat noodles in fish-based coconut milk curry gravy (Johor laksa style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00KQ used as closest parent for noodle dish in spiced coconut-fish broth; no exact Laksa Johor code exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Laksa Johor as a specific food item.

main_ingredients: thick rice noodles or spaghetti-style noodles

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Laksa Johor weighs approximately 500g, comprising noodles, fish-coconut gravy, and fresh herb garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Laksa Johor — IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Malaysian Food Composition Database)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for Laksa Johor. IMR Malaysia is an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: University of Sydney GI Database — rice noodle and thick wheat noodle analogues (GI range 50–70)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Laksa Johor. GI of 62 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice noodles and thick wheat noodles (GI range approximately 50–70). Coconut milk fat content moderates the GI slightly. Glycemic load per 500g serving calculated as (62 × 74g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 45.9, rounded to 45.8 per input source. This is consistent with the high-GL classification for a full bowl.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content — 420mg per 100g; a 500g bowl delivers approximately 2,100mg sodium (87% of the 2,400mg daily limit), primarily from fish-based gravy, fish sauce, belacan, and sambal accompaniment.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Laksa Johor to once per week at most, leave a substantial portion of the gravy unconsumed to reduce sodium intake, and avoid or minimise the sambal accompaniment.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving — a 500g bowl delivers approximately 74g available carbohydrate with a GL of ~45.8, driven by the thick noodle base (GI ~62); coconut milk fat provides only modest glycaemic moderation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a half noodle portion with extra fish and herb components, monitor post-meal blood glucose carefully, and treat Laksa Johor as an occasional meal rather than a regular dietary choice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (4.2g per 100g; ~21g per 500g bowl) and elevated sodium (2,100mg per bowl) are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; however, Spanish mackerel provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and the saturated fat level is lower than richer coconut-milk laksa variants.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume Laksa Johor in moderation, limit gravy consumption to reduce both saturated fat and sodium, and favour the fish and herb components over the noodle-heavy portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Spanish mackerel provides omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin D (1.5mcg per 100g); fresh herb garnishes (daun kesum, bunga kantan) contribute anti-inflammatory phytochemicals; saturated fat is moderate at 4.2g per 100g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Laksa Johor is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis due to its omega-3 and anti-inflammatory herb content, though sodium intake from the gravy should still be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Laksa Johor is the distinctive laksa variant of Johor state in southern Malaysia, most famous for an unusual eating style: the thick, round noodles — or sometimes spaghetti-like rice noodles — are twirled around a fork in a Malay adaptation of pasta-eating. The gravy is a rich fish-coconut milk curry base made with flaked Spanish mackerel (tenggiri) simmered in a thick, spiced coconut milk broth seasoned with lemongrass, galangal, chilli, turmeric, and belacan (shrimp paste). Unlike Singapore's prawn-coconut laksa or Penang's tamarind-sour asam laksa, Laksa Johor is defined by its fish-forward, coconut-rich gravy and its generous fresh herb garnish of daun kesum (Vietnamese coriander / laksa leaf) and bunga kantan (torch ginger flower), served alongside sliced cucumber and sambal. Per 100g, Laksa Johor provides 138 kcal, 7.2g protein, 6.5g fat (4.2g saturated from coconut milk), and 14.8g carbohydrate. A standard 500g bowl delivers approximately 690 kcal, 74g carbohydrate, and 2,100mg sodium. The glycemic index is approximately 62 (medium), but the glycemic load per bowl is high (~45.8), making it a concern for type 2 diabetes management. The sodium content is a significant concern for hypertension. The fish component contributes omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin B12 (1.2mcg/100g), niacin (3.2mg/100g), and selenium (14mcg/100g). Laksa Johor is halal and dairy-free. It is a culturally significant dish in Johor state and is closely associated with cross-border food culture between Johor Bahru and Singapore.
common_user_queries: laksa johor vs penang asam laksa

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD (Institute for Medical Research Malaysia), which is an approved primary regional source for Malaysian dishes under Project Veritas v2. The source entry is an exact match for Laksa Johor. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using rice noodle and thick wheat noodle analogues, which is the approved GI source. All core macros are complete and internally consistent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Laksa Johor not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry as of the accessed date. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for this dish. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD source — no industrial trans fats in traditional fish-coconut laksa preparation). All other nutrient fields are populated from the IMR Malaysia MyFCD verified source. GI value of 62 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice noodles and thick wheat noodles (GI range 50–70); no direct GI measurement for Laksa Johor exists in the published literature. Glycemic load per 500g serving calculated as (62 × 74g) / 100 = 45.88, consistent with input source value of 45.8. This dish is a Malaysian Malay specialty of Johor state and is distinct from Singapore Laksa (prawn-coconut) and Penang Asam Laksa (tamarind-sour fish). The multilingual naming rules applied are those for Malaysian dishes (Malay mandatory); Chinese, Tamil, and Indonesian aliases are included for regional retrieval coverage given the cross-border cultural significance of this dish.
